fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a lightweight notification dispatch thing built out. The idea is that our app code should be able to trigger toast-style alerts without knowing anything about how they get rendered — basically a middleman that takes a request and broadcasts it as an event. Think of it like the pub/sub pattern we used for that modal coordination work a while back, same general philosophy.

The service needs to handle a few different flavors: standard messages at different urgency levels (you know, the usual spectrum from info to critical), and also richer notifications where you're dropping in a whole custom view widget with some optional config params. For that second type, there's some nuance around when you attach a parameter bag vs. not — it's a bit like the conditional attachment logic we discussed in the last sprint review, so dig around the existing patterns if you're unsure.

Also needs dismissal support — users are complaining that stale banners stick around too long during page transitions. Should be able to wipe everything, wipe by urgency, or wipe just the widget-style ones.

The whole thing should be wired up so the adapter layer reads commands from stdin as JSON and spits results to stdout, one field per line. Invalid inputs for the widget-style notifications should surface a clean error rather than blowing up. We'll need a test runner script too that loops through the case files and captures raw output.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: NotificationDispatchService.show(level, message, message_kind, heading, on_click) / show_component(component, with_parameters, with_settings) / clear_level(level) / clear_all() / clear_custom()

trigger_keywords: what happens when heading is omitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no heading is supplied in a show request, the heading field defaults to an empty string — it must still appear in the output as 'heading=' (empty value), never omitted entirely.

The two message-supply forms — plain text ('message_kind'='text') and deferred render callback ('message_kind'='fragment') — are observationally equivalent. A message body is always considered attached regardless of which form is used, so the output always reports 'message=present'. There is no case where message is absent.

There are two strict attachment rules for the parameter bag in show_component: (a) when neither a parameter bag nor settings are supplied, an empty parameter bag is still attached and reported as 'parameters=present'; (b) when ONLY settings are supplied (no explicit parameter bag), no parameter bag is attached and output reports 'parameters=absent'. In other words, settings-only requests suppress the default empty bag.

When the component argument is not a valid component type, the core must raise a domain-level error (not a generic exception). The adapter catches it and normalizes output to exactly one line: 'error=invalid_component_type'. No event is raised, no other output lines are emitted.

The 'timeout' and 'show_progress_bar' lines are conditional — they ONLY appear when settings were attached ('settings=present'). When settings are absent, those two lines must not be emitted at all. The values use their natural types: timeout is an integer (e.g. 'timeout=2'), show_progress_bar is lowercase boolean (e.g. 'show_progress_bar=true').

The click handler is reported as 'on_click=present' when on_click=true was passed, and 'on_click=absent' otherwise (including when the field is omitted entirely). It is never reported as 'on_click=true' or 'on_click=false' — always use the present/absent vocabulary.

The output field order is strictly defined per event type. For 'show': event, level, heading, message, on_click. For 'show_component': event, component, parameters, settings, then (if settings present) timeout, show_progress_bar. For 'clear_level': event, level. For 'clear_all' and 'clear_custom': just the event line. Deviating from this order will fail the contract.

The test harness entry point is 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (default: 'public_test_cases'). For each case it writes raw stdout to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt' — zero-padded 3-digit case index, no PASS/FAIL metadata, just the raw program output.
The four valid severity levels are exactly: Info, Success, Warning, Error — with this exact casing (title case). These are used verbatim in output lines like 'level=Info' or 'level=Warning'.

trigger_keywords: clear custom vs clear all difference
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are three distinct dismissal operations that each raise separate events: 'clear_level' (scoped to a severity, carries the level in output), 'clear_all' (removes everything, no payload, outputs only 'event=clear_all'), and 'clear_custom' (removes only component-bodied notifications, outputs only 'event=clear_custom'). These three must not be collapsed into fewer events.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'show', 'show_component', 'clear_level', 'clear_all', 'clear_custom'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the pub/sub pattern we used for that modal coordination work a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The publish/subscribe hub architecture described in start.md where producers call the dispatch service which raises events that a separate display surface subscribes to — core logic in the NotificationDispatchService raises typed events, adapter subscribes and formats stdout output

context_pointers.vague_reference: the conditional attachment logic we discussed in the last sprint review
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two parameter bag attachment rules in Feature 2 (show_component): empty bag attached by default when no params and no settings; bag suppressed when only settings are provided — see feature2_show_component.json cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: the usual spectrum from info to critical
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The four severity levels: Info, Success, Warning, Error — 'critical' is NOT a valid level name; the correct top-severity label is 'Error'

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the existing patterns in the codebase and align with the overall pub/sub philosophy of the service. The key principle is that producers and the renderer never reference each other directly, and the adapter owns all stdout formatting.

feature1_show_notification.json.description: A notification dispatch service raises a show event whenever a notification is requested at a given severity level. The request carries a message body supplied either as plain text or as a deferred render callback, an optional heading (empty when none is given), and an optional click handler. The emitted output reports the routed severity level, the heading slot value, whether a message body is attached, and whether a click handler is attached. The message body is attached in every case regardless of how it was supplied, and the two message-supply forms behave identically.

cases.expected_output:
event=show
level=Info
heading=Account updated
message=present
on_click=absent

feature2_show_component.json.description: The dispatch service can request a custom component notification instead of a plain message. The request optionally carries a key/value parameter bag and optional display settings (a timeout and a progress-bar flag). The emitted output reports whether the component reference, the parameter bag, and the settings were attached, plus the settings values when present. Two behavioural rules hold: when neither a parameter bag nor settings are supplied, an empty parameter bag is still attached; when only settings are supplied, no parameter bag is attached. A request whose target is not a valid component type is rejected with a normalized error and raises no event.

cases.expected_output:
event=show_component
component=present
parameters=absent
settings=present
timeout=2
show_progress_bar=true

feature3_1_clear_by_level.json.description: The dispatch service raises a clear-by-level event when asked to remove all notifications of a given severity. The emitted output reports the clear event and the severity level that was requested, so the level routed through the event can be verified.

feature3_2_clear_all.json.description: The dispatch service raises a clear-all event when asked to remove every active notification regardless of severity. The emitted output reports that the clear-all event fired.

feature3_3_clear_custom.json.description: The dispatch service raises a dedicated clear-custom event when asked to remove all custom component notifications, separately from level-based and clear-all removal. The emitted output reports that the clear-custom event fired.
